item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think maybe if there were more emphasis on how it was arranging the debris, like maybe as an OCD person would, that would lend it a more definite sense of sentience.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Short and sweet. It's interesting at first glance, but it is indeed a bit bare. I also feel it has some tonal issues that I can't quite put my finger on, but they do not detract from the article. I'm leaning towards the positive with this one, but it still is very bare. Minimalism is it's own virtue, but it only goes so far. Still, it is strong enough to stand on it's own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: http://www.goominet.com/unspeakable-vault/vault/513/ This might be what happens to it in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, sentience merely requires senses, so if it can be established to respond to stimuli, that's sufficient.

Its self-sorting of different flotsam types could be construed as that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The containment procedures are just silly.

Flat-out. They just are. No -1, because I don't think it deserves it, but the containment needs a change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1'd on concept alone. Looking forward to more content being added. I agree that contact of some sort would justify suspicions of sentience, I would also like to see some kind of explanation as to why it is better for ships to be diverted from it.

I applaud the attempt tokeep it simple, this one could easily be muddled by minutia. But I think you took it a tad too far.

item.title: It's kind of missing something...
item.content:encoded: I think an addendum would really help this article out. It has potential, but just a few experiment logs would really brighten this piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All seagoing vessels that approach or enter the Gyre must be monitored; those vessels wishing to simply pass through the Gyre should be diverted.

From Wikipedia:

The North Pacific Gyre, located in the northern Pacific Ocean, is one of the five major oceanic gyres. This gyre covers most of the northern Pacific Ocean. It is the largest ecosystem on Earth, located between the equator and 50° N latitude, and comprising 20 million square kilometers.

How is Foundation going to keep vessels out of "most of the northern Pacific Ocean"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually like this one enough as it is, but if you so decide to add more, quikngruvn, let me say that if you add a cryptic nor from the thing that animated it, I'll downvote like there's no tomorrow. To me, the obvious way to add hook would be to set this a bit further in the future, as the Foundation starto work out how to talk to it…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to upvote this, but can't because of the whole "sentience" thing. All I see is anomalous currents making anomalous patterns. Nothing to indicate any kind of sentience. No vote unless that's removed, or there's a rewrite to actually indicate sentience. If that's not changed, It's probably going to be a downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is exactly what I was thinking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I initially downvoted as I perceived this to be little more than a sentient pile of garbage, but I think it's grown on me in the few days I've had to contemplate it. Therefore, I'm issuing a +1 of encouragement, but I still maintain that this article is in dire need of a hook and some addenda.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed, sentience requires proof, so far all it has is a suspected circulatory system. If the Foundation has never established communication, it wouldn't be called sentient.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why would any researcher leap from 'anomalous ocean currents' to 'OMG sentience!'?

The 'sentience' seems to be asserted by the article on the basis of nothing other than sci-fi cliche.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I would love if it explored the thoughts or expressions of such an entity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding my love for the idea and apathy for the execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Section of North Pacific Gyre gains sentience" is theoretically a good idea (edit: a really good idea), but this isn't going from that idea to an interesting skip. Not sure what it needs, but the base idea isn't enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks to all who have read, voted, and commented, and I am definitely seeing the prevailing view that there's not enough there there. And I can dig it— I don't want to make the article overly long and complicated, but at the same time, I can't just ignore a bunch of people saying it needs more (especially when no one's saying it needs less). Now just gotta ponder how and what to add/change. (I do have to admit that I'm glad this didn't immediately tank, but Roget was right in chat— I can do better than simply mediocre.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm upvoting because I love the idea, I just wish you did more with it. There isn't really a hook beyond it itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. Though I wonder if a bit more detail or emphasis could be given for why the Foundation concluded it's sentient, without losing the short and simple nature of the article.

Maybe it moves towards existing patches of common garbage, consuming them to increase in size? Or it's a threat to boats that enter its boundaries, causing anomalous currents or assaulting it with larger pieces of debris to capsize them and pull the flotsam under the surface, disintegrating it and sorting its component parts?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: im not saying this is bad but it seems alot of SCPs recently have been "so-and-so has developed sentience"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Short and sweet is something we definitely don't have enough of these days, so it's an admirable thing to try for on occasion. The problem with that is that you need to make sure that what you're writing about is interesting enough to carry the article merely by the fact of its own existence.

In this case, this just doesn't cut it. All I'm really seeing is currents in the Great Pacific garbage patch that make patterns and group stuff together. If that's it, I'm underwhelmed. I'm also unsure why they automatically think that this phenomenon means "sentient garbage patch". No possibility that something else is causing this? The mere existence of patterns doesn't really establish that this is a living sentient entity, in my view.

Also, you're working against the fact that the Great Pacific garbage patch is a horrifying phenomenon in real life that's mind-boggling to think about in terms of its scale and implications. Frankly, "sentient current that sorts things" pales in comparison to the real-world setting that you're working with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …no, 10,000 km2. Ten thousand square kilometers. That's a square 100 km on a side, or a circle with a diameter of (let me find my calculator) about 113 km. Small enough to easily fit between California and Hawai'i.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ehn, that's not an unfair assessment. In my head, someone managed to see the patterns (and remember, humans are good at pattern recognition), the Foundation learned about that observation somehow, and managed to run a bunch of tests that demonstrated that the organization has happening too often to simply attribute to chance. The subsurface particulate streams were discovered later, essentially confirming the existence of an anomaly.

That really was the hardest part to figure out, though: how the hell would the Foundation (or anyone) figure out there's more than just a bunch of floating garbage? I figured pattern creation and recognition were the way to go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've read this twice, and I still don't know what it is. It seems to be some kind of trash circulatory system or something. Also, it says the entity might be 10000 km across. Thats about the size of the entire pacific ocean. How would you keep ships away from that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: None whatsoever. Trying to keep this short and simple.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The frequency and complexity of the patterns that arise all but rule out creation by random chance, particularly in light of the other anomalous observation of SCP-2899.

Which is not detailed anywhere? This seems minimally anomalous, and strains credulity that the Foundation would even figure out that there's something going on other than weird currents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup. : 3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good simple stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that this holds its own despite the lack of any addendum or expansion. This is about the Great Pacific Garbage Patch, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, this one is fun! Although a bit bare. Any expansions planned here?
